2.1.1.4 Unique biological hazard of plutonium [50].
The main isotopes of plutonium, in order of decreasing importance are $^{239}\mathrm{Pu}$ , $^{240}\mathrm{Pu}$ , $^{241}\mathrm{Pu}$ , $^{238}\mathrm{Pu}$ , and $^{242}\mathrm{Pu}$ .

In high-burnup LMFBR fuel, Pu-241 presents the principal short-term hazard (Table 2.1-I). $^{239}\mathrm{Pu}$ emits 5.15 MeV alpha particles which range only 3.6 cm in air and less than $45\mu \mathrm{m}$ in water.
In each ionizing collision with air or water molecules, the alpha loses 35 eV.
After about 120,000 collisions, it has lost all of its kinetic energy.

3.3.1.9 Summary: We seek maximum $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ content and hard spectrum in the fuel salt and a softer spectrum in the outer blanket.
3.3.2.1 Neutron moderating effect.
Thus chlorine $(A = 36)$ will produce a much harder neutron spectrum than fluorine $(A = 19)$ , satisfying a principal core guideline.
3.2.2.2 Neutron absorption.
F absorbs significantly less than Cl (Table 3.3-VI), which further recommends F for the blanket.
Numerous workers have suggested enriching the chlorine in $^{37}\mathrm{Cl}$ as $^{35}\mathrm{Cl}$ contributes most to absorption at thermal energies.

However, despite the fact that $^{37}\mathrm{Cl}$ has the low level density of a magic nucleus ( $N = 20$ neutrons), its S-wave strength function ( $\Gamma_{n}^{o}/D$ ) and gamma width $\Gamma_{\gamma}$ will be near the same at fast neutron energies.
Thus no immediate evidence exists that enrichment in $^{37}\mathrm{Cl}$ will reduce the fast neutron absorption.

3.3.2.7 Cost and availability.
The power needed for pumping the core salt depends directly on its density; heat transfer can the other hand increases with $\rho^{0.8}$ .
Thus, lead $(10.5\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^3)$ requires high pumping power; sodium $(0.83\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^3)$ , much less.
Typical densities reported for salts in the fluoride HsBR and chloride MCFR (Pu) concepts are 3.2 and $3.1\mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ respectively, indicating little difference due to halide choice.
3.3.3.1 Actinides inherent to an MCFR(Th).
Since only thorium crosses the plant boundary as feed replacement material, the principal actinides in the system will be Th and U isotopes with A-232-234.
Pa, Np, and Pu should total less than 1% (section 3.5.8.1).
Later reactors will start up on bred $^{233}$ U.

By itself UCl₄ corrodes, but not when UCl₃ predominates (i.e. when the stoichiometry is near 3 Cl atoms to 1 U atom).

3.5.4.1 Chains for actinide transmutation.

Figure 3.5-3 details the Th cycle burnup chain as it pertains to reactor criticality. $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ branching - by neutron capture to $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ or by $\beta^{-}$ decay to $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ - is very important in a thermal reactor where $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ only absorbs neutrons.
However, in a fast MCFR(Th) spectrum $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ mostly fissions (Figure 2.1-5) - much more so than any other threshold-fission isotope.

Production of $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ follows directly from $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ : $^{233}\mathrm{Th}$ decays quickly enough to ignore its presence. $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ decays at the rate of $\ln 2 / 27.0$ days = 3.0 x $10^{-7}$ disintegrations per second per $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ atom.

With $\phi = 10^{16} \, \text{n cm}^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ in core (25% of time), ~0 out-of-core, and a core-spectrum-averaged cross section of 0.3 barns (Table 3.5-V), $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}(n, Y)$ reactions occur 0.7 x $10^{-9} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ , more than two magnitudes slower than $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ decay.
A thermal reactor with $\sigma_{2200} = 39b$ and $\phi = 10^{14} \, \text{n cm}^{-2} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ x 0.25 yields a similar rate of 1 x $10^{-9} \, \text{s}^{-1}$ [131].

In summary, $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ production from $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ parallels that in a thermal reactor, but a low-threshold fission cross section raises it to fissile fuel status and also destroys it faster.

Therefore the reduced presence of $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ and the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ -like reactivity behavior of $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ in an MCFR obviate the need for separating out $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ to let it decay as in an MSBR.
Therefore it suffices in these survey calculations to ignore the $^{233}\mathrm{Pa}$ ( $n, \gamma$ ) branch, i.e. to construe all $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ captures to pass directly to $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
